Focus on discovering the essential knowledge that would help an AI agents be immediately productive in this codebase. Consider aspects like:
- The "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand - major components, service boundaries, data flows, and the "why" behind structural decisions
- Critical developer workflows (builds, tests, debugging) especially commands that aren't obvious from file inspection alone
- Project-specific conventions and patterns that differ from common practices
- Integration points, external dependencies, and cross-component communication patterns

Guidelines (read more at https://aka.ms/vscode-instructions-docs):
- If `.github/copilot-instructions.md` exists, merge intelligently - preserve valuable content while updating outdated sections
- Write concise, actionable instructions (~20-50 lines) using markdown structure
- Include specific examples from the codebase when describing patterns
- Avoid generic advice ("write tests", "handle errors") - focus on THIS project's specific approaches
- Document only discoverable patterns, not aspirational practices
- Reference key files/directories that exemplify important patterns

Update `.github/copilot-instructions.md` for the user, then ask for feedback on any unclear or incomplete sections to iterate.

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="apply_patch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **apply_patch**</summary>

**Input:**

- explanation: `Add copilot instructions file tailored to this React repo explaining architecture, workflows, and editing conventions.`
- input:

```
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: d:\Download\cn-study-guide\.github\copilot-instructions.md
+## Purpose
+
+Quick, actionable instructions to help an AI coding agent be immediately productive in this repository.
+
+## Big picture (what this repo is)
+
+- Single-page React app (Create React App / `react-scripts`). The entire study guide UI lives in `src/App.js` as a mostly-monolithic collection of small function components (e.g. `Row`, `Card`, `Tabs`, `ST`) and many topic sections (prefixed with `NL_`, `TL_`, etc.).
+- Entry point: `src/index.js` -> renders `<App />` into `public/index.html` `#root`.
+- No external UI libraries — styling is inline in JSX (objects passed to `style`), and diagrams are embedded SVG inside `App.js`.
+
+## Key files to reference
+
+- `src/App.js` — single source of truth for content and UI. Search for topic function names like `NL_IPv6`, `NL_Routing` to find examples.
+- `src/index.js` — app bootstrap.
+- `public/index.html` — root DOM, minimal head tags and global body background.
+- `package.json` — scripts and React dependency versions. Use `npm start`, `npm run build`, `npm test`.
+- `netlify.toml`, `vercel.json` — deployment rewrites/redirects (Netlify publishes `build/`, Vercel rewrites everything to `/`).
+- `README.md` — project overview and deployment hints; mirrors the app content and topic list.
+
+## How to run and validate quickly
+
+- Install: `npm install`
+- Dev server (hot reload): `npm start` → http://localhost:3000
+- Production build: `npm run build` (output folder: `build/`)
+- Tests: `npm test` (default CRA runner; there are no custom tests in repo)
+
+If you make only content/UI edits inside `src/App.js`, the dev server's hot reload will update the browser immediately. If you change deps or `package.json`, stop and restart the dev server.
+
+## Project-specific coding conventions
+
+- Content and UI live together in `src/App.js`. New topics should follow the pattern used there: define a small function component named for the topic (e.g. `NL_NewTopic`) and return JSX/card arrangements. Examples: `NL_IPv6`, `NL_Routing`.
+- Use inline style objects (do not add separate CSS files unless refactoring intentionally). Maintain existing color palette (common colors: `#0f172a`, `#1e293b`, `#94a3b8`, `#10b981`, `#0ea5e9`, `#f59e0b`) for visual consistency.
+- Diagrams are raw SVG inline inside JSX. Preserve `viewBox` and relative coordinates for responsiveness.
+- Keep functions small and pure (stateless where possible). Use `useState` only when the UI already uses it (e.g. Tabs, toggles) and follow existing patterns.
+
+## Adding or editing content (practical recipe)
+
+1. Open `src/App.js` and search for an existing section near similar topics (search `NL_`, `TL_`, `ST`, or `Tabs(`).
+2. Add a function component for the new content near other topic functions (example: `function NL_MyTopic(){ return <Card>...</Card>; }`).
+3. Wire it into the place that renders tabs or the main layout — look for the Tabs usage in the same file and add an entry `{id: 'mytopic', label: 'My Topic'}` and conditional render `view==='mytopic' && <NL_MyTopic/>` using the same `useState` pattern.
+4. Run `npm start` and verify layout, colors, and SVGs in the browser.
+
+Notes: this is a large single-file codebase; prefer small incremental edits. Large refactors (splitting into multiple files) are fine but should be done in a dedicated PR and verified manually.
+
+## Build, test, and deploy notes
+
+- CI / deploy platforms: Vercel and Netlify are both supported (see `vercel.json` and `netlify.toml`). Both expect a CRA build output in `build/`.
+- Netlify config includes a redirect to `index.html` for SPA routing — do not remove `netlify.toml` when changing routing behavior.
+- If adding dependencies, update `package.json` and include a lockfile for reproducible installs (recommended). After changing deps, run `npm install` and restart the dev server.
+
+## Debugging tips
+
+- Use browser devtools + React DevTools to inspect component tree (everything is inside `<App/>`).
+- Console logs inside `src/App.js` are sufficient for tracing — avoid adding global logging libraries.
+- SVG problems usually relate to JSX syntax (e.g., attribute names). Keep React-compatible attributes (`className`, camelCase style props if used).
+
+## Search shortcuts useful to agents
+
+- Find topic components: search for `function NL_` or `function TL_` in `src/App.js`.
+- Find tabs: search for `Tabs(` usage to see how sections are wired.
+
+## When to ask for clarification
+
+- If a change touches stateful navigation outside `src/App.js` or introduces a build dependency (webpack/babel change), ask for confirmation before proceeding.
+
+---
+If anything in these instructions is unclear or you want more detail (for example, a recommended refactor plan to split `src/App.js` into components), tell me which part to expand and I will update the file.
+
*** End Patch
```


</details></tool-use>

_**User (2026-06-20 15:29:01Z)**_

user@DESKTOP-JQKN2EA MINGW64 /d/Download/cn-study-guide (master)      
$ git push -u origin main
error: src refspec main does not match any
error: failed to push some refs to 'https://github.com/Nourin-nnn/cn-study-guide.git'


---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5-mini)**_

You're seeing that error because your local repo doesn't have a branch named main — you tried to push a ref that doesn't exist locally. You have a few safe options depending on what you want to do. Pick one and run the matching commands.

Quick checks (recommended)
```powershell
git status
git branch        # shows local branches
git branch -a     # shows local + remote branches
git remote -v     # shows origin URL
```

Options

1) Create remote branch main from your current branch (fast — leaves local branch name unchanged)
- Use this if you want to keep your local branch name (e.g. `master`) but create `main` on the remote.

```powershell
git push -u origin master:main
```
This pushes your local `master` to remote `main` and sets the remote `main` as the upstream for your local `master` (so future `git push`/`git pull` will use that upstream).

2) Rename your local branch to main and push (recommended if you want local branch named `main`)
- Use this if you want your local branch to become `main`.

```powershell
git branch -m main        # rename current branch to 'main'
git push -u origin main   # push and set upstream
```

3) If you intended to push to remote `master` (not `main`)
- Use this if your remote actually has `master` as the default and you simply mistyped `main`.

```powershell
git push -u origin master
```

4) If the remote already has a `main` and you want to track it locally
- Use this if `origin/main` exists and you want your local `master` to track it (be careful — merge may be required).

```powershell
git fetch origin
git checkout master
git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/main master
git pull                 # merge remote main into local master (resolve conflicts if any)
```

Notes and tips
- The error "src refspec main does not match any" always means Git couldn't find a local ref named `main`.
- After pushing with `-u` once, future pushes can be done with `git push` (no need to specify origin/branch).
- If collaborating, confirm with teammates (or check repo settings on GitHub) whether the canonical branch should be `main` or `master` before renaming.

If you tell me which outcome you want (create remote main, rename local to main, or push master to remote master), I can give a tailored sequence and explain any follow-up steps (e.g., updating GitHub default branch).
